name: Ringold
short_description: Ringold offers rockhounding along the Yakima River in central Washington's dry steppe, where silicified wood and colorful agates erode from Miocene formations. Beginner-friendly public land access ...
description:
Ringold is a small community in Franklin County along the Yakima River in south-central Washington, situated in the rain shadow of the Cascade Mountains. The area sits within the Pasco Basin, a geologically rich zone where volcanic and fluvial deposits from the Miocene epoch created excellent conditions for agatization and petrification. Public access along and near the river allows rockhounders to search for silicified wood, colorful jasper nodules, and chalcedony specimens that have been weathered from surrounding basalt-derived soils and ancient river deposits.

The terrain around Ringold is characterized by moderate slopes, sagebrush steppe vegetation, and intermittent draws leading to the Yakima River. The low elevation of 635 feet and semi-arid climate mean the land dries quickly after rare winter moisture, making spring and fall ideal collecting seasons. Water access and relatively gentle terrain make this location accessible to families and beginners, though sturdy footwear is recommended for the rocky, uneven ground.

The Yakima River valley has a long history of rockhounding activity, and Ringold's proximity to the water means fresher specimens regularly emerge from erosion. The river itself offers exposure of older geological layers, and the benches and draws above it yield scattered finds of petrified wood fragments, agate nodules with translucent banding, and jasper in shades of red, brown, and cream. This location is particularly rewarding after spring runoff or winter rains when new material has been exposed.
history: Ringold sits within the Pasco Basin, formed during the Miocene epoch when massive basaltic lava flows and intercalated sedimentary layers created a complex stratigraphy. Later fluvial activity from ancestral Columbia and Yakima river systems deposited silica-rich sands and gravels, which weathered over millions of years to produce the agatized wood, jasper, and chalcedony specimens found here today. The region's geology reflects the collision of tectonic plates and the subsequent development of the modern Yakima River drainage.
tips: Search the exposed banks and benches along the Yakima River where water erosion continuously reveals new material; older terraces above the river often produce complete petrified wood specimens and nodular agate. Bring a hand lens to examine color banding in agate nodules and identify gem-quality pieces; many finds here display dramatic concentric patterns in reds, browns, and creams. Look for chalcedony in translucent forms along seams in eroded basalt; these semi-transparent specimens often cluster in small draws where water drainage concentrates lighter materials. Early morning and late afternoon light makes spotting the gloss of agatized material easier against the dusty ground.
terrain_notes: Elevation 635 feet in the semi-arid Yakima River valley with moderate, rolling slopes. Vegetation is sparse sagebrush steppe with occasional riparian growth near the water. The area is relatively open with few obstacles, but terrain is uneven, rocky, and often loose underfoot. Hiking distance from parking to productive areas ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on river access point chosen. Footing is challenging in places due to volcanic cobbles and loose scree; sturdy boots with good ankle support are essential.
best_season: October - April. The semi-arid climate means summer heat (80-95 degrees Fahrenheit) can be intense with little shade. Fall through early spring offers cooler temperatures and occasional rain that freshens exposed material. Winter and early spring are prime for finding newly exposed specimens after water erosion.
written_directions: From Pasco, take Highway 395 north toward Reardan for approximately 8 miles. Turn east on Highway 260 and drive about 4 miles until you see the Ringold area and river access points. Look for informal pullouts along local roads that provide access to the Yakima River benches and draws. GPS coordinates 46.488451, -119.248572 mark a central area; approach via county roads and seek parking near river drainages where previous rockhounders have accessed the site.
road_conditions: Local roads are improved gravel and dirt in good condition. Main highways are paved. Approach roads to river access may be rough in places but generally passable by standard vehicles. Avoid during heavy rain when washouts are possible.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small parking areas along county roads near river access points. Space is limited; early arrival recommended during weekends. No developed parking facilities; expect 2-4 vehicle capacity at most pullouts.
collection_rules: Ringold is located on public land open to rockhounding for personal use. Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Pack out all waste. Respect private property boundaries clearly marked in the area. No collection within posted private sections along the river.
rules:

No motorized excavation

nearest_services: Pasco, approximately 8 miles south, has full services including gas, restaurants, grocery stores, and water filling stations. Reardan to the north also has basic services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain with uneven, rocky footing typical of river valleys. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Sturdy footwear with good ankle support essential due to volcanic cobbles and loose material. Walking distances from parking to active sites range 0.5 to 2 miles. Shade is minimal; hat and sun protection necessary.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding near Ringold, Washington?
faq.answer: Ringold is known for agate, jasper, petrified wood, and chalcedony. The agates here often display beautiful red, brown, and cream banding. Petrified wood fragments ranging from small pieces to larger specimens are common finds along the Yakima River benches. Chalcedony appears in translucent forms within eroded basalt seams.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Ringold, Washington, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Ringold is completely free and requires no permit. The area is public land open to personal use collection under FLPMA guidelines, which allow you to collect up to 25 lbs per day at no cost.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find agates at Ringold?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Ringold rockhounding. The cooler months mean less intense heat, and winter rains and spring runoff expose fresh material along the Yakima River. Summer heat can exceed 90 degrees with little shade, making fall and spring the most comfortable and productive seasons.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids rockhounding at Ringold, Washington?
faq.answer: Yes, Ringold is very family and kid-friendly. The terrain is relatively easy with gentle slopes, and there is no entrance fee or permit required. Children as young as 6 can enjoy collecting here, though sturdy shoes are essential due to rocky, uneven ground. The open landscape allows good supervision, and the Yakima River provides scenic views.

faq.question: How far is Ringold rockhounding area from Pasco, and what tools do I need?
faq.answer: Ringold is about 8 miles north of Pasco via Highway 395 and Highway 260. For collecting, bring a hand lens to examine agate banding, a small pick or flathead screwdriver for extracting specimens, and a collection bag. Sturdy boots, a hat, sunscreen, and water are essential due to the semi-arid terrain and exposure.

faq.question: Are there restrictions on rockhounding at Ringold, or can I use power tools?
faq.answer: Ringold allows personal use collection only, with no commercial use permitted. Motorized excavation and power tools are prohibited. You may collect up to 25 lbs per day by hand with hand tools like picks and hammers. Respect marked private property boundaries and pack out all waste.

meta_title: Ringold Rockhounding - Find Agate and Petrified Wood
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood at Ringold, Washington. Free public rockhounding near Pasco with easy access to Yakima River specimens.
